author: Augustine of Hippo
book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
Recognition and Form Belong to Souls as Well as Bodies.
title: Recognition and Form Belong to Souls as Well as Bodies.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: Chapter 21 [XV.]—Recognition and Form Belong to Souls as Well as Bodies.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: But you say: “If the soul is incorporeal, what was it that the rich man saw in hell?

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: He certainly recognised Lazarus; he did [not] know Abraham.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: Whence arose to him the knowledge of Abraham, who had died so long before?”

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: By using these words, I suppose that you do not think a man can be recognised and known without his bodily form.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: To know yourself, therefore, I imagine that you often stand before your looking-glass, lest by forgetting your features you should be unable to recognise yourself.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: But let me ask you, what man does anybody know more than himself; and whose face can he see less than his own?

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: But who could possibly know God, whom even you do not doubt to be incorporeal, if knowledge could not (as you suppose) accrue without bodily shape; that is, if bodies alone can be recognised?

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: What Christian, however, when discussing subjects of such magnitude and difficulty, can give such little heed to the inspired word as to say, “If the soul be incorporeal, it must of necessity lack form”?

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: Have you forgotten that in that word you have read of “a form of doctrine”?

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: Have you forgotten, too, that it is written concerning Christ Jesus, previous to His clothing Himself with humanity, that He was “in the form of God”?

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: How, then, can you say, “If the soul is incorporeal, it must of necessity lack form;” when you hear of “the form of God,” whom you acknowledge to be incorporeal; and so express yourself, as if form could not possibly exist except in bodies?
